title: Opposition Fractures as Özel Prepares New Party, Russia Blocks S-400 Exit, Lira Pressed Near 47
subhead: Ousted CHP leader Ozgur Ozel announced plans for a new party as the judicial recess deadline looms, Russia confirmed it will not accept return of Turkey's S-400 systems, and the lira continued its slide toward 47 per dollar amid elevated oil prices and geopolitical uncertainty.

sections.heading: Ozel Announces New Party Plans as CHP Crisis Hits Deadline
sections.body:
Ousted CHP leader Ozgur Ozel announced on live television that he is preparing to establish a new political party, with official steps expected around late July or early August. 'We are working on the name and logo. Let us call it the New Party for now,' Ozel told Sozcu TV, according to Bianet. The move comes as the opposition's legal options narrow ahead of the judicial recess beginning July 20.

Ozel's faction appealed the court decision that annulled the CHP's November 2023 congress and reinstated former leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu. If the appeals and extraordinary congress requests are not resolved before courts go into recess, Ozel said his camp would accelerate new-party efforts. He claimed 833 party delegates have joined demands for an extraordinary congress, but Kilicdaroglu's administration has refused to convene one.

Two parallel tracks are now in motion: one team is drafting bylaws, a program, and a founders' board for a new party, while another group is preparing an existing party as a vehicle, Ozel said. The split deepens an opposition crisis that began in May, when an appeals court voided Ozel's election and police stormed CHP headquarters to enforce Kilicdaroglu's reinstatement. The Conversation notes that 20 CHP mayors have been detained on corruption and terrorism-related charges, and Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu — the CHP's 2028 presidential candidate — has been imprisoned since March 2025 facing sentences of over 2,000 years.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's largest opposition party is on the verge of a formal split, which would fragment the electoral force that made historic gains in the 2024 local elections. The judicial recess deadline creates a hard cutoff that could lock in Kilicdaroglu's contested leadership for months.

sections.heading: Russia Blocks S-400 Return, Complicating F-35 Path
sections.body:
International relations analyst Thrasyvulos Eftychidis told parapolitika.gr that Russia remains the primary obstacle to resolving Turkey's S-400 impasse. 'Russia does not accept their return. Nor does it agree to their transfer to another country without its consent,' Eftychidis said, noting that Kremlin spokesman Peskov's latest comments indicate the matter 'will be examined' but no authorization has been granted — particularly for a potential sale to a Gulf state.

Greek Deputy Foreign Minister Alexandra Papadopoulou confirmed to parliament that 'the US has made no commitment to supply Turkey with F-35s,' adding that foreign policy should be judged on 'tangible facts, not impressions,' according to Indicator.gr. She was responding to a parliamentary query about the potential sale's impact on Aegean military balance.

Greek Defense Minister Nikos Dendias said Athens 'would not welcome' Turkey receiving either F-35s or F110 engines for Kaan fighters, and demanded that any NATO weapons sale include guarantees they not be used against a fellow alliance member. 'Providing such a platform without the condition that it cannot be used against another allied state raises the question of whether that truly serves American interests,' Dendias said at The Economist's government roundtable in Athens, the Jerusalem Post reported.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has publicly urged the US not to provide weapons to Turkey, calling Erdogan someone who 'deserves absolutely nothing' and noting Israel's qualitative military edge designation. Meanwhile, former Turkish Defense Minister Hulusi Akar, now chair of parliament's defense committee, held meetings in Washington with House Armed Services Committee Chairman Mike Rogers and Rep. Pat Harrigan to push for S-400 resolution, Indicator.gr reported.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's refusal to approve S-400 transfer is the single biggest obstacle to Turkey's F-35 return. Even if Trump lifts CAATSA sanctions, the 2020 NDAA requires Turkey to no longer possess the S-400 before F-35 delivery can proceed — and Russia holds veto power over where the systems go.

sections.heading: Congress Divided but Skeptical on CAATSA Relief
sections.body:
President Trump's announcement that he would lift CAATSA sanctions on Turkey faces significant congressional resistance, Jewish Insider reported. Rep. Mike Lawler (R-NY) called an F-35 sale 'not in the best interest of the United States,' while Rep. Nicole Malliotakis (R-NY) listed Erdogan's alignment with Iran and Russia, harboring of Hamas, and occupation of Cyprus as reasons to block the deal. Rep. Brad Sherman (D-CA) warned lifting sanctions would be 'tantamount to handing Putin the blueprints to our technology.'

However, a bipartisan Senate delegation at the NATO summit signaled cautious openness. Sen. Jeanne Shaheen (D-NH), who co-authored the legislation kicking Turkey out of the F-35 program, said that 'if there is an acceptable way to deal with the S-400,' returning Turkey to the program 'would be a positive development.' Sen. Mike Rounds (R-SD) echoed that position. Rep. Mike Turner (R-OH) said the details shared with Congress 'do appear to be promising.'

The legal pathway is narrow: the 2018 waiver provision requires the administration to certify that a waiver would not endanger US alliances and that Turkey is actively decreasing reliance on Russian equipment, with reports to Congress every 90 days. Eighteen House Democrats, led by Rep. Dina Titus (D-NV), sent a letter to House leaders urging them to block the F-35 move through a joint resolution of disapproval.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's announcement was a political signal, but the CAATSA statute gives Congress real leverage to block or delay the move. The 90-day certification cycle and bipartisan skepticism mean the F-35 question will be fought over for months, not resolved quickly.

sections.heading: Erdogan Backs Iran Deal, Warns Israel, Courts Pakistan
sections.body:
President Recep Tayyip Erdogan warned Saturday that Israel should not be allowed to undermine the US-Iran interim agreement, telling a joint press conference with Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif in Istanbul: 'We are closely following the Israeli administration's attempts to dynamite the US-Iran deal,' according to Reuters.

Erdogan referred to the Islamabad Memorandum — the interim understanding between the US and Iran mediated by Pakistan — and praised Sharif's role in brokering it. Arab News reported that Sharif signed the memorandum as mediator after it was signed by Trump and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian.

The two leaders also discussed deepening bilateral cooperation in energy, transportation, critical minerals, IT, and defense, with a bilateral trade target of $5 billion. Turkish Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar said Turkish companies want to contribute to energy projects in Pakistan, Reuters reported.
sections.whyItMatters: Erdogan is leveraging the post-ceasefire diplomatic landscape to position Turkey as a regional arbiter. The Pakistan pairing — a nuclear-armed state that mediated the Iran deal — gives Ankara a channel to claim influence over the Hormuz settlement without direct involvement in the negotiations.

sections.heading: Lira Presses Toward 47 as Oil and Geopolitics Weigh
sections.body:
The Turkish lira continued its managed depreciation, with USD/TRY inching toward 47.00, InvestingCube reported. The lira remains in a structural depreciation regime, with the CBRT actively managing the pace to prevent disorderly moves.

Three factors are driving pressure: elevated domestic inflation that remains well above the CBRT's long-term target; higher oil prices — Brent crude at $87/barrel as of mid-July, up from just above $70 before the US-Iran conflict, according to Al Jazeera — which hit Turkey hard as a net energy importer; and safe-haven dollar demand driven by geopolitical risk aversion that pulls capital away from emerging-market assets.

The CBRT, through Governor Fatih Karahan, has stated that deterioration in inflation expectations 'remains limited' despite the geopolitical backdrop, according to Anadolu Agency. The bank has maintained tighter monetary conditions and taken policy steps to restore confidence in its framework, though analysts note the lira's trajectory remains fundamentally bearish barring a geopolitical resolution.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's current account deficit widens directly with oil prices, and the CBRT has limited room to defend the lira without further draining reserves. A sustained oil price above $85/barrel would add 1-2 percentage points to headline inflation, making the CBRT's disinflation trajectory harder to maintain.

sections.heading: Iraq-Syria Pipeline and Hormuz Bypass Momentum Build
sections.body:
Iraq and Syria signed a memorandum of understanding to rehabilitate the Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline, which has been largely out of service since the 2003 US-led invasion of Iraq, i24 News reported. Chevron will oversee the project, with a consortium including Chevron, Capital TI, and Qatar's UCC conducting technical and feasibility studies. The route would provide a Mediterranean outlet for Kirkuk crude, reducing Iraq's dependence on the Strait of Hormuz.

Iraq's production fell more than 50% in June to 1.9 million barrels per day, down from 4.2 million bpd in February before the US-Iran conflict, CNBC reported. A US State Department official confirmed Washington is supporting the pipeline reconstruction and expects US companies to play a role.

The pipeline deal is part of a broader regional push: the UAE plans to double export capacity outside Hormuz via a second pipeline to Fujairah, Saudi Arabia is considering expanding its Red Sea pipeline by 2 million bpd, and Goldman Sachs estimates regional pipeline capacity could grow to over 14 million bpd by end-2028 — more than 60% of pre-war export volume. However, analysts note pipelines remain vulnerable: Iran struck a Saudi pumping station in April, slashing throughput by 700,000 bpd.
sections.whyItMatters: For Turkey, the Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline revives a route that once fed Iraqi crude through Turkish territory. If rebuilt, it would strengthen Ankara's hand as an energy corridor state — but the route runs through Syrian territory now controlled by a post-Assad government whose stability is uncertain.

bottomLine: Ozgur Ozel's announcement of a new party marks the formalization of Turkey's opposition split, with the judicial recess deadline of July 20 effectively freezing the CHP crisis in Kilicdaroglu's favor for months. On defense, Russia's refusal to approve S-400 transfer is the harder constraint on F-35 return than CAATSA sanctions alone — and Israel and Greece are building a coordinated blocking coalition in Washington. The lira's slide toward 47 reflects an uncomfortable reality: Turkey's inflation fight is being undermined by oil prices that its geography cannot shield it from, and the CBRT's tools are mostly about managing the pace of decline, not reversing it. The Iraq-Syria pipeline deal, backed by Chevron and Qatar, signals that the Hormuz shock is producing durable infrastructure changes — but Turkey's role in the new energy map depends on whether Syrian territory remains stable enough for a pipeline to operate.
sources: Bianet, Jewish Insider, Al-Monitor, Indicator.gr, Jerusalem Post, Khaama News/Reuters, InvestingCube, Al Jazeera, CNBC, i24 News, The Conversation, Anadolu Agency, PageNews.gr, DevDiscourse, Daily Sabah
